coredata.dc:title: Adaptative blue-light bio-inspired visual nociceptor using an all-in-one chlorophyll-derived CQDs sensitized 2D semiconductor toward human’s myopia prevention
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Zheng, Lei
Ren, Jianzhou
Liu, Yanan
Hu, Yongxu
Guo, Hongling
Han, Yemei
Wang, Yuchan
Wang, Zhongwu
Zhang, Kailiang
Hu, Wenping
coredata.dc:description: Adaptative blue-light bio-inspired visual nociceptor (BIVN) system holds the potential to perceive noxious blue-light stimuli and further provide early-warning and/or risk avoidance for human eyesight. However, it is highly challenging to integrate functions such as outstanding blue-light detection under an illumination below the eye’s tolerance threshold, photo-memory, and complex pain computation capabilities into a single device. This integration typically requires distinct functional components for each performance metric, resulting in complex fabrication processes and limiting overall performance. Here, we present an adaptative blue-light BIVN based on leaf-derived chlorophyll-functionalized carbon quantum dots (Chl-CQDs) sensitized MoS2, featuring a “reverse” type-II heterostructure and highly integrated all-in-one functionality. By leveraging the synergistic absorption mechanism and charge-self-trapping behavior within the mixed-dimensional heterostructure, a significant enhancement of approximately 100,000 times in detectivity (D*) and photoresponsivity (R) below the eye’s tolerance threshold blue-light illumination ∼450 lx. Also, it demonstrates reliable photo-memory and computational capabilities for implementing essential BIVN functions, firstly showcasing exceptional adaptive behaviors with a neural blocking degree ∼35 % in a 2D-based VN system. This work may offer a new paradigm for concurrently sensitizing and manipulating charge transport within 2D materials to expand their potential in advanced neuromorphic visual systems.
coredata.dcterms:subject: 2D semiconductor
“reverse” type-II heterostructure
chlorophyll-derived CQDs
Blue light
Bio-inspired visual nociceptor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Adaptative Chl-CQDs/MoS2-based blue-light bio-inspired visual nociceptor (BIVN). a) Schematic of pain production and transmission in body system, and the difference in blue-light intensity between the received by the eyes and its perception. b) Artificial blue-light BIVN based on a “reverse” type-II hybrid memory phototransistor, using the sycamore leaf-derived chlorophyll-functionalized carbon quantum dots (Chl-CQDs) sensitized MoS2 as the semiconductor layer. The device shows the essential characteristics of blue-light-excited corneal VN and the visual pain adaptative ability. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Assembly and characterization of Chl-CQDs/MoS2 hybrid structure. The a) optical image of MoS2 crystals with a large size of 100 ± 3 μm, b) AFM image of MoS2 crystals with a thickness of 1.35 ± 0.04 nm, c) SAED patterns and d) Raman of single-crystal MoS2. e) The photograph of dispersed Chl-CQDs in ethanol solution, synthesized by sycamore leaves with a simple and environment friendly hydrothermal process. f, g) Schematic illustration of the fabrication process for Chl-CQDs sensitized MoS2 crystals through spin-coating technology. h) Typical AFM image of Chl-CQDs/MoS2 heterostructure. i) The HRTEM images of the capsule-shaped Chl-CQDs and their internal CQDs by utilizing the spin-coated Chl-CQDs film. The CQDs typically exhibit a diameter ranging from 2 nm to 5 nm and display lattice fringes with spacings of approximately 0.2 nm and 0.32 nm. j-i) Typical FTIR, UV–vis absorption spectra and PL spectra of the bare MoS2 crystals, Chl-CQDs film and Chl-CQDs/MoS2 hybrid structure.

Characterization of charge traps in Chl-CQDs/MoS2 heterostructure. a) The estimated “reverse” type-II energy band alignment between MoS2 and Chl-CQDs before and after contact, which the built-in electric field (Ebi ) could be formed at the Chl-CQDs/MoS2 interface. b) Transfer curve of bare MoS2 and Chl-CQDs/MoS2 hybrid structure. c) Extracted memory window (left axis) and calculated capacity factor (the γ is defined as the ratio of hysteresis-induced voltage window (ΔV) and applied maximum VG bias, right axis) as a function of VG,
max. d) The cyclability of the Chl-CQDs/MoS2-based devices with memory function. e, f) Comparison of XRD spectra and thermally stimulated current (TSC) measurement between bare MoS2 and Chl-CQDs/MoS2 heterostructure.

Device performance of Chl-CQDs/MoS2-based heterotransistors. a) Schematic illustration and optical image of the hybrid photodetector under 450 nm blue light excitation. The scale bar is 50 μm. b) The possible synergistic absorption mode of Chlorophll and CQDs. c) Charge-transport mechanism of the “reverse” type-II Chl-CQDs/MoS2 heterostructure. d) The I-V curves before and after sensitization for the MoS2 single-crystal OPTs, and Chl-CQDs film tested at 35.9 μW cm−2 and VG = 0 V. e) Corresponding magnification times (β) in R and D* of above two-terminal device without VG modulation. f, g) Typical transfer curves of bare MoS2 and Chl-CQDs/MoS2 phototransistors at different light power densities conditions. The device channel length and width are 20 μm and 80 μm, respectively. h) The values of P, R and D* after sensitization for the MoS2-based tested at different light illumination. i) The calculated β in P, R and D* before and after sensitization for the MoS2-based devices tested at different VG conditions and 35.9 μW cm−2 light illumination. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

One to one correspondence of a) the visual nociceptor (VN) in the human eyes’ system and our developed blue-light BIVN with different working modes at VG > VT and VSD > 0, including b) weak blue-light illumination (below threshold illumination of 56.5μW cm−2), c) strong illumination (over threshold illumination), and d) nociceptive blockade and visual pain adaptation under the strong blue-light illumination (over threshold illumination). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Chl-CQDs/MoS2-based blue-light BIVN integrated nociceptive blockade and self-protection abilities. a) The threshold property of the Chl-CQDs/MoS2-based BIVN measured at varied pulsed blue light (450 nm, pulse duration: 1 s) intensity from 19.7 to 250.2 μW cm−2. b) The response of the BIVN under pulse amplitudes = 19.7 μW cm−2, 56.5 μW cm−2 and 87.2 μW cm−2. c)The relaxation characteristic of the device by applying strong (56.5 μW cm−2)-weak 36.5 μW) cycles light with different interval times (interval time = 5 s, 10 s, 20 s). d) The training pulses for sensitization property with amplitudes from 19.7 to 250.2 μW cm−2 followed by the single injury pulses of 0 μW cm−2 (blue), 165.6 μW cm−2 (red) and 250.2 μW cm−2 (green). e, f) Allodynia and hyperalgesia characteristics after experiencing different degrees of injury. g) Nociceptive blockade of the device stimulated by using 20 continuous pulses. h) Statistical results of the nociceptive blocking degree with varied light power and pulse widths. i) Threshold elevation of this device after experiencing different degrees of nociceptive blockade, known as the visual pain adaptation. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Adaptative blue-light bio-inspired visual nociceptor using an all-in-one chlorophyll-derived CQDs sensitized 2D semiconductor toward human’s myopia prevention

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Tianjin Key Laboratory of Film Electronic and Communication Devices, School of Integrated Circuit Science and Engineering, Tianjin University of Technology, Tianjin 300384, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Tianjin Key Laboratory of Film Electronic and Communication Devices
School of Integrated Circuit Science and Engineering

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Organic Integrated Circuits
Tianjin Key Laboratory of Molecular Optoelectronic Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Organic Integrated Circuits, Ministry of Education, Tianjin Key Laboratory of Molecular Optoelectronic Sciences, Institute of Molecular Aggregation Science, Tianjin University, Tianjin 300072, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shenzhen Key Laboratory of New Information Display and Storage Materials, College of Materials Science and Engineering, Shenzhen University, Shenzhen 518060, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shenzhen Key Laboratory of New Information Display and Storage Materials
College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Tianjin Key Laboratory of Electronic Materials and Devices, School of Electronics and Information Engineering, Hebei University of Technology, Tianjin 300401, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Here, we present a pioneering adaptive blue-light bio-inspired visual nociceptor (BIVN) enabled by all-in-one chlorophyll-derived CQDs (Chl-CQDs) sensitized MoS2, which features a “reverse” type-II heterostructure. It exhibits an ultra-high sensitizing gain of approximately 100,000 times in D* and R, reliable memory behavior, and complicated visual pain computational capabilities. This is due to the novel synergistic absorption and charge self-trapping mechanism within Chl-CQDs/MoS2 mixed-dimensional heterostructure. The study presents a novel paradigm for concurrently sensitizing and manipulating defects within 2D materials to enhance their potential for application in artificial neural sensory systems.

Adaptative blue-light bio-inspired visual nociceptor (BIVN) system holds the potential to perceive noxious blue-light stimuli and further provide early-warning and/or risk avoidance for human eyesight. However, it is highly challenging to integrate functions such as outstanding blue-light detection under an illumination below the eye’s tolerance threshold, photo-memory, and complex pain computation capabilities into a single device. This integration typically requires distinct functional components for each performance metric, resulting in complex fabrication processes and limiting overall performance. Here, we present an adaptative blue-light BIVN based on leaf-derived chlorophyll-functionalized carbon quantum dots (Chl-CQDs) sensitized MoS2, featuring a “reverse” type-II heterostructure and highly integrated all-in-one functionality. By leveraging the synergistic absorption mechanism and charge-self-trapping behavior within the mixed-dimensional heterostructure, a significant enhancement of approximately 100,000 times in detectivity (D*) and photoresponsivity (R) below the eye’s tolerance threshold blue-light illumination ∼450 lx. Also, it demonstrates reliable photo-memory and computational capabilities for implementing essential BIVN functions, firstly showcasing exceptional adaptive behaviors with a neural blocking degree ∼35 % in a 2D-based VN system. This work may offer a new paradigm for concurrently sensitizing and manipulating charge transport within 2D materials to expand their potential in advanced neuromorphic visual systems.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In the age of electronic information explosion, the high-energy blue light with a wavelength ranging from 410 nm to 455 nm generally exists in various electronic equipment. Moreover, the human's eye is typically unable to detect the harm that blue light causes to our eyes because the number of cone cells that perceive blue light in the eye is much smaller than that of green light. Hence, it can easily cause various eye diseases such as decline in visual acuity even the retinal degeneration when it passes through the cornea into the eye at high intensity or for long hours of blue-light stimulation.[1–5] The visual nociceptor (VN) is a key sensory receptor in eye’s system that can recognize noxious light stimuli, which in turn generates and delivers the warning signals to the central nervous system. Once detection of noxious stimuli or stimulus intensity exceeds a certain threshold level, the VN would transmit pain signals to the visual cortex of the brain for high-level processing to avoid potential damages.[6,7] Thus, reliably mimicking the retinal functionalities by using a blue-light bio-inspired visual nociceptor (BIVN) represents a significant advancement in realizing eyes’ health monitoring and disease treatment.

Neuromorphic 2D-based photoelectronic synapses hold the potential to realize artificially engineered BIVN due to their unusual electric and optical properties.[8–16] They can resolve the issue of traditional complementary metal oxide semiconductor (CMOS) based multifunctional devices often accompanied by elaborate circuitry and power-extensive.[9,10] However, it presents a significant challenge in achieving BIVN at the single-unit level of 2D devices and/or materials, there is a necessity to integrate high-efficiency photo-sensing, photo-memory and visual pain computation. In general, to enhance the photoresponse of 2D-based phototransistors, those consisting of colloidal quantum dots (QDs) containing heavy metals coupled with 2D semiconductor materials via functioned heterostructures attributed to their high extinction coefficient and size-tunable bandgap.[17–19] Moreover, to achieve memory function for better simulate bio-inspired visual processing, some works have introduced electret,[20] floating-gate structure[21] or artificial defect centers/obstacles[22] into above 2D device construction. The preparation of these devices, however, often pose challenges in terms of control, complex structure and biological incompatibility. Additionally, they only allow the acquisition of visual information as an artificial photoreceptor,[23–25] neglecting their pain-perceptual and pain-adaptive capabilities, which hamper the effective triggering of motor responses upon recognizing noxious light stimuli and subsequently providing warnings to protect the body from further harm. On the other hand, to more accurately simulate the multi-modal bionic behaviors of VN associated with blue light-induced human myopia, the forthcoming blue-light BIVN needs to efficiently operate within the eye’s tolerance illumination threshold ∼450 lx or 66 μW/cm2,[26] which remains a great challenge in current 2D-based neuromorphic optoelectronic devices.

Herein, to address the aforementioned challenge, we developed an adaptive blue-light BIVN, employing a heterostructure consisting of single-crystal MoS2 sensitized by chlorophyll-derived carbon quantum dots (Chl-CQDs) extracted from sycamore leaves. It innovates in terms of that: i) We use a simple and environmentally friendly sensitization strategy to significantly enhance the blue-light photoresponse of 2D semiconductors. Below an eye’s tolerance threshold blue-light illumination ∼35.9 μW/cm2, the key photoelectric parameters of Chl-CQDs/MoS2 in R and D* are improved by over 100,000 times than pristine MoS2 device. ii) The reliable memory function, characterized by good cyclability and a high on/off ratio of approximately 104, is also demonstrated in the designed “reverse” type-II Chl-CQDs/MoS2 heterostructure. iii) Based on i and ii, we further obtain an adaptative 2D-based blue-light BIVN simultaneously integrating photo-sensing, photo-memory, nociceptive perception and blockade functions of visual pain. Due to an enhanced charge self-trapping effect within the heterostructure, as the photoresponse contribution of the device gradually saturates, a maximum blocking degree of up to 35 % is demonstrated under a blue-light pulse of 280 μW cm−2 in the 2D-based VN system. These results demonstrate that our developed artificial BIVN offers significant advantages in the early detection and prevention of potential risks to human eyesight.

In the biological eye model depicted in Fig. 1 a, visual cognition is achieved through the processing of optical stimuli directly captured by the bipolar cells of the eye, which selectively establish connections with sensitive cones and rod receptors in the retina. For instance, the specialized lesser blue bipolar cells (BBCs) selectively contact the short-wavelength sensitive cones.[27] Then, the generated bio-chemical voltaic signals are transferred by retinal ganglion cells to the optical nerve and finally are transmitted into the visual cortex of the brain for further processing. Based on the luminous flux Eq. (1):[28]
(1)
Ψ v
=
K m
∫
380
780
ϕ e
( λ )
d λ
where the Ψv is the luminous flux, Φe is the watts per nanometer, λ is the wavelength, V(λ) is the spectral luminous efficiency function and Κm is the maximum spectral luminous efficacy. It reaches the largest value at 555 nm and gets the relatively low sensitive value after wavelength shorter than 480 nm, it reflects the human's eye is the most insensitive to the short-wavelength blue light maybe due to lesser BBCs in eye’s system. Therefore, the device with the capacity of efficiently detecting and perceiving the harmful short-wavelength blue light is very important for human visual health. Inspired by such biological behaviors and application requirements, we design a high-performance blue-light BIVN based on a “reverse” type-II Chl-CQDs/MoS2 heterostructure (Fig. 1b). Such a device, which integrates sensing, memory, and computation capabilities, not only implements fundamental BIVN functions but also exhibits essential adaptation and self-protection abilities.

MoS2 single crystals are selected as the semiconductor layer due to their excellent photoelectric figures of merit and strong blue-light absorption. As showed in Fig. S1, high-quality MoS2 crystals are directly prepared on an octadecyltrichlorosilane (OTS) modified SiO2 (300 nm)/Si substrate by using a chemical vapor transport (CVT) method. The optical (Fig. 2 a) and AFM images (Fig. 2b) show that the MoS2 crystals generally have a large size over 100 μm and thickness as low as 1.35 nm, respectively, demonstrating a typical bi-layer structure. The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pasterns of MoS2 crystals display a uniform morphology over the entire selected area and had distinct lattice fringes with a-spacing ∼3.2 Å, suggesting its highly single crystalline property. The spots in the hexagon in Fig. 2c can be indexed as (100) and (010) well corresponding to the lattice constants of MoS2 crystals. Moreover, the MoS2 is also identified by Raman spectroscopy (Fig. 2d). The E2g peak (387 cm−1) in the Raman spectrum is the in-plane vibrational mode of MoS2, and the A1g peak (404 cm−1) is the interlayer vibrational mode of MoS2.[29].

The production process of Chl-CQDs is shown in Fig. 2e and Fig. S2. Specifically, the fresh sycamore leaves are firstly cleaned, dried, and ground into powder by a grinder, and then scattered into anhydrous ethanol to perform solvent extraction. Subsequently, the chlorophyll-based ethanol solution is reacted into the poly (tetrafluoroethylene), centrifuged at room temperature, and filtered by a molecular sieve to purify the products. The solvent removals through rotary evaporation and drying under vacuum. At last, the purified product is dissolved in friendly anhydrous ethanol to form a target capsule-shaped Chl-CQDs solution. The synthesis process is detailed in the experimental section. Fig. 2f and g illustrate the fabrication processes of Chl-CQDs/MoS2 hybrid structure, which the capsule-shaped Chl-CQDs composite dissolves in friendly ethanol solution is directly assembled on the pre-grown MoS2 single crystals. The 3D image of AFM (Fig. 2h) clearly shows that the capsule-shaped Chl-CQDs successfully assembled onto the MoS2 crystals. To well understand the composite structure of Chl-CQDs, the morphology of the as-prepared capsule structure Chl-CQDs is performed by HRTEM (Fig. 2i), which exhibits the nanoparticles packaged in the capsule matrix. The sizes of single capsule structures are generally more than 50 nm, where almost all these internal quasi-spherical nanoparticles have a diameter of 2 nm ∼5 nm. Additionally, highly resolved lattice fringes with a spacing of approximately 0.2 nm and 0.32 nm are observed in high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), which correspond to the graphene facet (100) and the interlayer spacing between two sets of stacked graphene facets with a lattice fringe distance of 0.2 nm, respectively. The results demonstrate the formation of crystalline carbon dots structure (CQDs).[30].

The EDS element mappings (C, N, O, Mg) of Chl-CQDs are exhibited to demonstrate the existence of chlorophyll in the capsule matrix, as shown in Fig. S3. The distribution of the Mg element is like the N element and exhibited the vacant sites with a similar size to the CQDs. It indicates that the chlorophyll is distributed around the CQDs and still retains N-based light absorption sites in the capsule matrix. The FTIR spectroscopy of Chl-CQDs (Fig. 2j) shows the IR bands at about 3200 cm−1-3600 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of O-H, which come from ethanol-induced solvothermal method. The peak at 2920 cm−1 is corresponding to the C-H stretching vibrations of aliphatic groups. Stretching vibrations of C=O groups from chlorophyll molecules with conjugation, and C=C are located at about 1750 cm−1 and 1620 cm−1, respectively. The existence of stretching vibration bands at 1500 cm−1-1360 cm−1 demonstrates the existence of C-C and C-N groups in the pyrrole ring of chlorophyll. The peak at about 1040 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibrations of C-O from the chlorophyll or the alcoholic C-OH.[30] The C=C group band appears at about 1620 cm−1 implying the formation of CQDs, which is further supported by the high-resolution scan of C1s region by XPS measurement (Fig. S4). The C1s region can be resolved into five distinct peaks at approximately 288 eV, 286.1 eV, 285.4 eV, 284.8 eV, and 284.3 eV, corresponding to O-C=O, C-O-C, C-O/C-N, C=C, and C-C bonds respectively. The carboxylic acid peak occurs at 288 eV in Chl-CQDs due to the presence of the ester bond. Meanwhile, the O1s peak of Chl-CQDs can be deconvoluted into three single peaks at about 535.1 eV, 533.8 eV and 532.3 eV, which are attributed to O-H, O-C=O and C=O, respectively. These findings demonstrate the role of solvothermal reaction in the formation of ethanol-derived hydroxyl functionalities.[30] The above results demonstrate the capsule composite structure integrated the groups of CQDs and chlorophyll.

Impressively, the capsule-shaped Chl-CQDs enhance all the main absorption peaks of the MoS2 original absorption at about 428 nm, 604 nm, and 653 nm, as showed in Fig. 2k. According to reports, the absorption peak around 250 nm ∼270 nm is resulted from the π-π* transitions of C=C group in graphene and CQDs.[31] Therefore, the absorbance peak of the Chl-CQDs at 251 nm can be attributed to the π-π* transition of the C=C bond. The chlorophyll, being a typical magnesium porphyrin derivative, possesses an extensive π-electron system that facilitates electronic transitions between π to π* orbitals. This results in the formation of large absorption bands around 600 nm ∼800 nm and 380 nm ∼450 nm.[32] The absorbance peaks of the Chl-CQDs around 380 nm and 673 nm are like the sycamore leaf chlorophyll extracted by ethanol (Fig. S5), which evidence the existence of the chlorophyll. Moreover, the main peaks of Chl-CQDs was significantly enhanced than all the chlorophyll original absorption, indicating the synergistic absorption between the chlorophyll and CQDs. Hence, the combination of CQDs and chlorophyll in capsule-shaped Chl-CQDs can more effectively induce absorption enhancement in 2D MoS2 semiconductors, showing great potential in fabricating ultrasensitive blue-light photo-detector.

The memory function plays a crucial role in the fundamental characteristics of photoexcited corneal nociceptors.[35,36] Fig. 3b and Fig. S7 illustrate the difference of hysteresis memory window in the transfer curve and transconductance along with VG between the Chl-CQDs/MoS2 heterostructure and pristine MoS2 single-crystal device. In general, a large hysteresis window in Chl-CQDs/MoS2 device usually means abundant carriers can be captured and released,[37]demonstrating the successful construction of traps by the “reverse” type-II heterostructure. Additionally, the observed negative shift of the threshold voltage (VT ) and corresponding transconductance level(Fig. S7 at a more negative VG provides further evidence of increased electron traps within the device channel of Chl-CQDs/MoS2. Fig. S8 shows the hysteresis curves measured at different VG conditions, in which the varied transfer characteristics and controllable memory window demonstrate the reproducibility and tunability of such devices. Extracted memory window and calculated capacity factor (γ) from above mentioned transfer curves as a function of VG,
max are shown in Fig. 3c, in which the γ is defined as the ratio of hysteresis-induced voltage window (ΔV) and applied maximum VG bias. The values of ΔV and γ increase linearly for widening ranges of the VG which the γ can reach up to 0.36 at a 40 V sweeping range. We then conducted an endurance test and observed a reproducible switching for over 300 cycles in Fig. 3d, the devices can exhibit good cyclability and retain a high on/off ratio of nearly 104 with advantages in nonvolatile memory applications. Compared to pristine MoS2 devices, almost all of the hybrid devices exhibit a slight decrement in on-state channel current due to the injection of opposite charges into MoS2. However, the heterostructure maintains favorable field-effect properties and has demonstrated a more excellent photo-detection performance under light excitation.

The defect states of Chl-CQDs/MoS2 are characterized and quantified to verify that the charge traps are effectively constructed. Fig. 3e shows the diffraction (XRD) spectra of pure MoS2 and Chl-CQDs/MoS2. Pure MoS2 has strong diffraction peaks at 25.88° and 14.31° respectively, indicating its single-crystal structure. In comparison, the two main diffraction peaks of pure MoS2 slightly right shift with ignored decreased crystallization after sensitizing by the capsule-shaped Chl-CQDs, which the peak of 25.88° changed to 25.95° and 14.31° changed to 14.38° (Fig. S9), respectively. It indicates that the defects of sensitized MoS2 increase mainly due to the fact that injected holes from Chl-CQDs to MoS2 and further forming the charge traps for n-type semiconductors.[38] Moreover, as shown in Fig. 3f, the thermally stimulated current (TSC) testing[39] temperature range of 300–340 K covered the thermal excitation energies of the charges captured by both intrinsic traps of pure MoS2 and Chl-CQDs-introduced deep traps. The TSC spectra of pure MoS2 and Chl-CQDs/MoS2 illustrate the two characteristic peaks of the current release. In comparison to pure MoS2, the current peaks of the Chl-CQDs/MoS2 slightly shift to a higher temperature and are significantly increased in intensity (depolarization current from 0.27 to 0.68 μA). Defects produced in injected holes into MoS2 process introduce charge traps (charge-self-trapping behaviors), which can be arguably identified by the increased TSC peak intensity, showing that more charge traps have been introduced by Chl-CQDs. The results demonstrate the physical mechanisms of charge-self-trapping behaviors within such a “reverse” type-II heterostructure, and thus leading to a reliable memory function in Chl-CQDs/MoS2-based hetero-transistor.

Fig. 4 a illustrates a circuit schematic and optical image of the MoS2/Chl-CQDs-based bottom-gate-top-contact (BGTC) heterotransistor. According to the FTIR results (Fig. 2j), the carbonization and hydroxyl-induced-condensation could make the assembly of the CQDs surface and chlorophyll outlying substituents via C-C/C-O/C-N bonds. These function bonds can induce new energy levels further produce new electron transitions at the edge states of CQDs, and further build the high-density distribution of CQDs in the chlorophyll matrix.[40] It facilitates the energy transfer among CQDs and chlorophylls in the capsule Chl-CQDs structure (Fig. 4b). The capsule-shaped Chl-CQDs with multiple π-electron systems exhibit a synergistic absorption mode, the photogenerated electrons shift from the Chl-CQDs to MoS2 layer under the strong electric field (Fig. 4c), thereby offering a new mechanism to enhance the photoresponse of MoS2. Considering the intrinsic Ebi , which can also facilitate the separation of photo-generated carriers,[41] the significantly enhanced photoresponse of MoS2 arises from the synergistic effect between the absorption properties of Chl-CQDs and Ebi . To experimentally investigate the excellent photoelectric performance of the heterostructure, we conduct current-curve tests on

pristine single-crystal MoS2, Chl-CQDs film and hybrid devices under both dark and illuminated conditions (blue light, 35.9 μW/cm2) with a VG of 0 V (Fig. 4d), respectively. Fig. S10 and the extracted amplification factor (β) in Fig. 4e display the changes in key parameters among the above three devices, including detectivity (D*) and photoresponsivity (R). The hybrid device exhibits significant enhancements in D* and R of over 700 times and 1300 times at a small voltage, respectively. The above parameters can be evaluated using follows Eqs. (2) and (3):[42]
(2)
D
∗
=
RA
1 / 2
2 q |
I
dark
1 / 2
(3)
R =
I
light
-
I
dark
P
inc

where Idark is the drain current under darkness, Ilight is the photocurrent, Pinc is the incident laser power density, S is the effective channel area of the device. q is the elementary electronic charge, and dark current density, respectively. In addition, we examine the photodetection performance of MoS2/Chl-CQDs heterotransistors with VG modulation to extend their great advantages in smart optoelectronic integration applications. Typical photo-induced transfer curves of both devices at different light power densities and bias conditions are plotted in Fig. 4f and Fig. 4g. The heterotransistor has stronger photoresponse dynamics effects than the bare MoS2 devices due to the significant increase in channel current. Based on the photocurrent measurements of the devices, the highest photosensitivity (P) value can be calculated as 2.1 × 104 and the maximum D* value reached 3.8 × 1012 Jones (blue light; 35.9 μW/cm2; VG = -5 V, Fig. 4h). The P is defined as the value of (I light-Idark)/I dark.43 The maximum R ∼over 1300 A W−1 is achieved when the blue-light intensity is applied at a very small 35.9 μW cm−2, which is one of the best records among the low-density blue-light photo-detection (Table S1). Fig. S11 and Fig. 4i show the changes in key parameters before and after sensitization for the MoS2 single-crystal phototransistor under 35.9 μW/cm2 conditions. Compared to the two-terminal device, the three-terminal hybrid phototransistor exhibited an amazing improvement in P, D*, and R of over 100,000 times more than the bare device, attributed to the photoelectric cooperation controlling mechanism.[43,44] Moreover, we evaluate the device stability under continuous operation in a weak (Fig. S12). The transfer curves of the device under a blue-light intensity of 35.9 μW cm−2 exhibit better consistency in one week. Notably, an acceptable offset in the curves after 3 days is observed which is resulted from the possible agglomeration effect of Chl-CQDs. These results compellingly demonstrate the high efficiency of our developed sensitization method while also highlighting its significant potential for detecting blue light below the human eye's tolerance threshold, thereby facilitating better simulation of eye-like behaviors and potentially preventing myopia-related eye diseases.

The VN system in the human eyes’ system with different physiological responses is shown in Fig. 5 a, which mainly includes the below threshold state and over threshold state under different intensity (or time) blue-light stimulus. The VN behavior is generally accomplished by the close collaboration between photoreceptors and electroreceptors. The output excitatory postsynaptic currents (EPSC) generated by photoreceptors are subject to significant regulation by electrical stimuli received from the electroreceptors when it is transmitted to the biological coding computing systems.[33] To better understand the working mechanism of Chl-CQDs/MoS2-based BIVN, one-to-one correspondence of the VN system in the human body and our developed artificial nociceptor with different working modes are shown in Fig. 5b-d.

Firstly, three key features (undamaged case) of a normal VN, namely “threshold”, “no adaptation”, and “relaxation”,[25,33,34] are experimentally demonstrated through blue-light pulse measurements for the Chl-CQDs/MoS2-based BIVN (Fig. 6 a-c). The triggering mechanism is highly dependent on the illumination intensity, illumination time, and the number of light stimuli. As such, we use 450 nm blue-light pulses of different amplitudes, pulse widths, and interval times to emulate electronic equipment’s stimuli for our artificial BIVN. It is worth pointing out that during these single pulse measurements, a suitable interval time (1 s) is used in between so that the device has enough time to relax back to its pristine state. When a train of light pulses is applied to the device at VG > VT, the EPSC response is highly dependent on the amplitudes of pulses it experiences (Fig. 6a). Generally, the device doesn’t show a relatively large current until the blue-light pulse amplitude reached a threshold state of 56.5 μW cm−2 (corresponding to Fig. 5b). Further increase of the amplitude to 87.2 μW cm−2 results in a larger EPSC because more photo-generated electrons dominated the channel current. The abundant electrons mainly result from the intrinsic photoresponse properties of MoS2, absorption properties of Chl-CQDs and Ebi facilitated photo-generated electrons (Fig. 5c). Fig. 6b shows trains of different amplitudes (19.7, 56.5 and 87.2 μW cm−2) and the corresponding EPSC pulses. It is observed that the initial current jump occurred after a certain period of time, which becomes shorter with a higher amplitude of the pulses because the photodynamic effect of carriers becomes more significant under a larger blue-light stimulus. The aforementioned statement once again demonstrates the dependence of the device's threshold property on the amplitude of blue light. In addition, applying a long enough time of light pulses will sustain the current level without relaxation or degradation, indicating that the response intensity does not change or decrease with the repeated input of the same noxious stimuli. This phenomenon is similar to the “no adaptation” characteristic of a VN, and is critical for the eye system of a human or a humanoid robot to protect itself against repeated harmful stimuli. After the noxious blue-light stimuli is removed, the BIVN starts the “relaxation” process. As shown in Fig. 6c, blue-light pulse measurements with different interval times (5 s, 10 s, and 20 s) are performed. The device is turned on with a first pulse (56.5 μW cm−2), and experiences a second pulse (36.5 μW cm−2) that comes after different interval times. If the same stimulation that normally does not turn on the device (e.g., 5 s/10 s pulse) is applied before the device relaxes (20 s, blue curve in Fig. 6c), a larger current response is observed (green and red curves in Fig. 6c, lower panel). The observed phenomenon is reminiscent of the heightened sensitivity exhibited by the Chl-CQDs/MoS2 device during the relaxation process, which can be attributed to a weakened photoresponse property and an increased injection of anti-type charges into the MoS2 layer over an extended interval time. This characteristic also plays a crucial role in early myopia prevention for humans.
Sensitization is a mechanism for a visual nociceptor to protect an injured area by enhancing the pain sensitivity to noxious light stimuli.[34] It can be characterized by

“hyperalgesia” and “allodynia”, referring to an increased response to a normally painful stimulus and pain resulting from a normally innocuous stimulus, respectively. To demonstrate the “sensitization” feature of Chl-CQDs/MoS2 BIVN, we first apply light pulses with high amplitudes (165.6 and 250.2 μW cm−2, 1 s width) to the devices, introducing a change that emulated the injury or damage to the VN system. The EPSC under different input blue-light power is recorded for devices that have experienced different levels of “damages” (0 μW cm−2, 165.6 μW cm−2 and 250.2 μW cm−2) (Fig. 6d). It shows that the BIVN with seriously “injured” has a higher EPSC resulted from enhanced photoresponse property and more photo-generated electrons injected into the channel at a higher light power illumination. The maximum EPSC at different input light powers is plotted in logarithmic and linear scale (Fig. 6e and Fig. 6f). Interestingly, the threshold shifted towards the lower end while the EPSC shifted higher. This suggests that a smaller threshold illumination is required to turn on a more seriously injured device, reproducing the allodynia and hyperalgesia characteristics in our BIVN device. In dangerous conditions, the eye-like devices with such VN features can respond at once and protect themselves from further damage, prominently improving the ability to cope with different levels of risk in the real world.

When a strong light stimulus is applied to the eyes, TWIK-related K+ channel 2 (TREK-2) located on the presynaptic membrane becomes activated, facilitating the release of K+ into the synaptic cleft and thereby inducing hyperpolarization at the presynaptic membrane.[45] This intervention inhibits pain signal transmission and mitigates neuronal damage within the biological system resulting from excessive neural signal expression (Fig. 5d, nociceptive blockade). Impressively, our developed BIVN not only shows basic VN functions but also demonstrates additional passivation and self-blockade behaviors when the number of stimuli increases further. As shown in Fig. 6g, a set of continuous pulses with an amplitude of 280.7 μW cm−2 and a width of 3.5 s are applied. The EPSC first goes up as the conducting channel quickly forms in response to a high pulse amplitude stimulus, showing its sensitization properties. After that, the EPSC slowly decreased with ongoing pulse application, mainly due to an enhanced Ebi that leads to more charge traps in MoS2, which is accompanied by nearly saturated photoconductivity performance in this device. It significantly differs from the saturated current trend observed during the inadaptation stage, indicating that a blockade function is activated, which may mimic nociceptive blockade in biological neural systems. Here, we use |Imin − Imax |/Imax to calculate the blocking degree, and Imin/Imax represents the minimum/maximum of EPSC within 20 pulses. The blocking degree is monotonically increased according to the pulse amplitude and width with the maximum blocking degree up to 35 % (Fig. 6g and h, 280.7μW cm−2, 3.5 s). In addition, after experiencing blockade, the nociceptive perception is retested and it shows a different threshold property (Fig. 6i). The threshold illumination with EPSC ∼11.7nA is about 280 μW cm−2, which is higher than 56.5 μW cm−2 (without blockade), showing the changing threshold property at the different levels of blockade which is well known as the visual pain adaptative behaviors. These interesting results firstly demonstrate a simulation of the nociceptive blockade behavior in the 2D-based artificial BIVN. By contrast, a neural blocking degree of biological nociceptors closer to 100 % is considered more effective. Although the study falls some way short of ideal biological nociceptors behavior, we believe that through further material innovation and structural design, the ideal value can be achieved by using our developed technology.

At last, we characterized the important parameters of our developed 3 × 3 device array, including amplification factor (β) of R, D*, and blocking degree, which exhibited good consistency among the devices (Fig. S13). Moreover, our developed BIVN, which is compatible with manufacturing processes for flexible substrates, has unique advantages in integrating into flexible or wearable devices and in exploring the application of machine learning in advanced biological vision systems, making it worthy of further investigation. However, it is worth mentioning that the prepared device maybe has the following shortcomings: Due to the potential degradation of Chl-CQDs during prolonged exposure to blue light, the electrical and optical properties of the BIVN device may degrade. While the transfer curves of the device under blue-light illumination show good consistency over a one-week period, the long-term stability requires further investigation. Alternatively, to enhance the stability of the BIVN device, either more stable quantum dots could be utilized for sensitization or a protective packaging layer could be implemented to isolate the device from the external environment. In addition, the hybrid structure of Chl-CQDs/MoS2 is fabricated by dissolving capsule-shaped Chl-CQDs composites in an ethanol solution and directly assembling them onto pre-grown MoS2 single crystals. Therefore, minimizing the influence of solvents on semiconductor materials is a critical issue that needs to be addressed and optimized in real-world manufacturing processes.

The capsule-shaped Chl-CQDs were synthesized by a solvothermal reaction of sycamore leaves. The fresh sycamore leaves were firstly cleaned, dried, and ground into powder by a grinder, and then 1.0 g powder was scattered in 20 mL anhydrous ethanol. Subsequently, the chlorophyll-based ethanol solution was transferred to a poly (tetrafluoroethylene) lined autoclave (50 mL) after solvent extraction and they was performed in an oven at 150 °C for 3.5 hrs. After cooling down to room temperature, the solution was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min and filtered through molecular sieve to purified the products, which the solvent removal via rotary evaporation and drying under vacuum. The pore size of the molecular sieve is 0.2 µm and the Chl-CQDs solution is further purified with the molecular sieve after the centrifuge purification. At last, the purified product was dissolved in anhydrous ethanol to form a capsule-shaped Chl-CQDs solution for further device fabrication. MoS2 single crystals were grown on OTS-treated /SiO2/Si substrate by a chemical vapor deposition (CVD) method. Based on a two-zone horizontal tube furnace, the MoO3 was placed in high-temperature zone at 750 °C, and the S powder was placed in the low-temperature zone at 180 °C. After rising to the target temperature at a rate of 20 °C/min, the MoS2 single crystals were grown on OTS-treated SiO2/Si substrate for 25 min under a high purity argon at a 100 sccm flow rate and 1 Torr pressure.

Optical microscopy images were taken with a Nikon ECLIPSE Ci-POL polarized optical microscope with a blue filter. Intelligent mode AFM was performed by using a Bruker Dimension Icon. Photoluminescence (PL) and Raman spectra were recorded on a HITACHI F-7000 spectrofluorometer. SAED patterns of MoS2 single crystal were measured by a Themis 300 cryo-transmission electron microscope. UV–Vis spectra were obtained on SHIMADZU UV-3600 UV–vis-NIR spectrophotometer. X-Ray diffraction was measured in reflection mode at 45 kV and 200 mA with Cu Kα radiation using a RIGAKU SMARTLAB9KW diffractometer. UPS (KRATOS Axis Ultra DLD spectrometer) data were obtained using He I (h = 21.22 eV) as the excitation source with a base pressure > 2 × 10-9 torr. XPS of Chl-CQDs and MoS2 crystals were measured by Escalab 250Xi. Energy-dispersive spectrometry (EDS) characterization was performed on an EDAX Octane Plus Det operating at 1.76 keV. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy measurements were carried out on a Thermo-Nicolet iS50 FTIR spectrometer.

MoS2 transistors with BGTC configuration were firstly fabricated by thermally deposited 35 nm Au on the MoS2-OTS-treated /SiO2/Si substrate as the source/drain electrodes. The patterned Au electrodes were fabricated through a manually aligned shadow mask with a substrate temperature of 23 °C and a vacuum of 1 × 10-6 torr. Subsequently, the Chl-CQDs in ethanol solution was spin-coated on the MoS2 single-crystal devices at a speed of 2000 rpm. for 10 s, and the sample was then annealed at 65 °C for 20 min to improve neck connection and therefore carrier mobility within Chl-CQDs. All the measurements of OFETs were tested on an Agilent B1500 semiconductor parameter analyzer with micromanipulator 6150 probe station at room temperature in the glove box.

Lei Zheng: Writing – original draft, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Jianzhou Ren: Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Yanan Liu: Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Yongxu Hu: Supervision, Project administration, Investigation, Funding acquisition. Hongling Guo: Investigation. Yemei Han: Investigation. Yuchan Wang: Supervision, Project administration, Investigation. Zhongwu Wang: Investigation. Kailiang Zhang: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Wenping Hu: Supervision, Funding acquisition.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: DPA-MoS2 van der waals heterostructures for ambipolar transistor and wavelength-dependent photodetection, ACS
